---
title: "querychat"
subtitle: "An example of a 'safe' and verifiable AI app"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, theme.scss]
    slide-number: c/t
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    incremental: false
    transition: fade
    code-line-numbers: false
---

## 

<script src="https://fast.wistia.com/player.js" async></script>
<script src="https://fast.wistia.com/embed/at01coepkh.js" async type="module"></script>
<style>wistia-player[media-id='at01coepkh']:not(:defined) { background: center / contain no-repeat url('https://fast.wistia.com/embed/medias/at01coepkh/swatch'); display: block; filter: blur(5px); padding-top:58.76%; }</style>

<wistia-player media-id="at01coepkh" aspect="1.701851851851852"></wistia-player>


## Three tools: visualize, query, filter

::: columns
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](images/querychat-viz.png){width="100%" fig-align="center"}
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
1. **Visualize:** execute ggsql queries, rendered as visualizations in the chat.
:::
:::

## Three tools: visualize, query, filter

::: columns
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](images/querychat-query.png){width="100%" fig-align="center"}
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
1. **Visualize:** execute [ggsql](https://ggsql.org/) queries, rendered as visualizations in the chat.
2. **Query:** execute SQL queries, rendered as tables/text in the chat.
:::
:::


## Three tools: visualize, query, filter

::: columns
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](images/querychat-filter.png){width="100%" fig-align="center"}
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
1. **Visualize:** execute ggsql queries, rendered as visualizations in the chat.
2. **Query:** execute SQL queries, rendered as tables/text in the chat.
3. **Filter:** execute filter SQL queries on predetermined views, allowing the LLM to reactively drill into the data in response to user questions.
:::
:::


## Get started

::: columns
::: column
```{.python filename="app.py"}
from querychat import QueryChat
from querychat.data import titanic

qc = QueryChat(
  titanic(), 
  "titanic",
  client="bedrock-anthropic",
  tools=("filter", "query", "visualize"),
)
```
:::

::: column
1. Supply a [data source](https://posit-dev.github.io/querychat/py/data-sources.html), LLM client, and tools to enable.

::: fragment
2. `QueryChat` auto-generates a system prompt with schema context and instructions for the LLM.
:::

::: fragment
3. Users can also provide a [data dictionary](https://posit-dev.github.io/querychat/py/context.html#data-dictionary) to further guide the LLM.
:::
:::
:::


## The pre-bundled app

::: columns
::: {.column width="40%"}

```{.python filename="app.py"}
from querychat import QueryChat
from querychat.data import titanic

qc = QueryChat(...)

app = qc.app()
```
<br>

```bash
shiny run app.py
```

:::

::: {.column width="60%"}
![](images/querychat-titanic.png){width="100%" fig-align="center"}
:::

:::

::: fragment
Useful for your own exploration, but for sharing with others, you'll probably want to build a **custom dashboard**.
:::


## A basic custom dashboard

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{.python filename="app.py"}
from shiny.express import render, ui
from querychat.express import QueryChat
from querychat.data import titanic

qc = QueryChat(titanic(), "titanic")
qc.sidebar()

with ui.card():
    @render.data_frame
    def data_table():
        return qc.df()
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
* This is essentially what `qc.app()` does under the hood.

::: fragment
* `qc.sidebar()` places the chat in a sidebar.
:::

::: fragment
* `qc.df()`: a **reactive** value containing the filtered dataset.
:::

::: fragment 
* Shiny provides best UX, but you can also use Streamlit, Dash, or Gradio.
:::

:::

:::

## Exercise {.center background-color="#0F3B5D"}

1. Open 


Give them an `.app()` to run? Make them trigger each of the three tools? Make them add a custom tool?

## Built-in tools {.center}

| Tool | What it does | User sees |
|---|---|---|
| **filter** | `SELECT * WHERE ...` — updates the dashboard | Data table + title update |
| **query** | Arbitrary analytics SQL — returns results | SQL + result table in chat |
| **visualize** | SQL + chart spec — renders inline | Altair chart in chat |

::: {.fragment}
```{.python}
# Default: filter + query
qc = QueryChat(df, "data")

# All three
qc = QueryChat(df, "data", tools=("filter", "query", "visualize"))
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
All tools are **read-only** — no INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE.
:::

::: notes
Walk through the three tools:

1. **filter** is the most common — the user says "show me rows where X",
   and the dashboard filters. The LLM writes a SQL WHERE clause, querychat
   validates and executes it, and `qc.df()` updates reactively.

2. **query** is for analytics — "what's the average tip by day?" The LLM
   writes full SQL, the result appears as a table in the chat.

3. **visualize** is opt-in (requires `pip install "querychat[viz]"`). It uses
   a SQL extension that generates Altair charts.

All three are read-only by design. The SQL is validated before execution.
:::

## Tools in action {.center}

::: {.placeholder style="min-height: 450px;"}
SCREENSHOT: querychat Titanic app showing a conversation —<br>
1. User asks "show me first-class passengers who survived" → dashboard filters<br>
2. User asks "what was the survival rate by class?" → query result table in chat<br>
(capture a real session to show the natural flow)
:::

::: notes
If time permits, do this live instead of a screenshot. The key thing the
audience should see: the user types natural language, the LLM writes SQL, and
the result appears either in the dashboard (filter) or inline (query). The
user never sees or writes SQL — but they could inspect it if they wanted.
:::

## Context management {.center}

querychat auto-generates context from your data:

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

### Auto-generated
- Column names and SQL types
- Numeric ranges (min/max)
- Categorical values (≤20 unique)

_The LLM knows your schema without seeing your data._

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

### User-supplied
```{.python}
QueryChat(
    df, "titanic",
    data_description=(
        "survived: 1 = survived, "
        "0 = did not survive"
    ),
    extra_instructions=(
        "Use British spelling. "
        "Refuse off-topic questions."
    ),
)
```

:::
:::

::: notes
This is the "context engineering" theme paying off. Auto-generated context
means the LLM understands the shape of the data (what columns exist, what
values are valid) without ever seeing the raw rows. This is both a
**correctness** benefit (the LLM writes valid SQL) and a **privacy** benefit
(no data leaks to the provider).

User-supplied context adds domain knowledge the schema alone can't convey:
what does `survived=1` mean? What tone should the assistant use? This goes
in `data_description` (about the data) or `extra_instructions` (about behavior).

Both can be inline strings or paths to markdown files.
:::

## The security model {.center}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

### ❌ The naive approach

Send your CSV to the LLM.

- Data **leaves your server**
- Doesn't scale past ~100K rows
- No reproducibility
- No verification

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

### ✅ The querychat approach

LLM writes SQL. You execute it locally.

- Data **stays on your server**
- Scales via DuckDB
- SQL is **reproducible**
- SQL is **verifiable** and **read-only**

:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
The LLM is a **translator** (language → SQL), not a **database**.
:::

::: notes
This is the philosophical payoff of the whole day. Remember slide 1 of the
foundations section: "focus LLMs on what they're good at (language → code),
engineer around the bad." querychat does exactly this.

The LLM never sees the actual data rows (except query tool results). It sees
the schema context, writes SQL, and querychat executes it in a local DuckDB
instance. The SQL is validated (read-only, no DDL) before execution.

This means:
- You can use querychat with sensitive data (it doesn't leave your server)
- You can use it with large datasets (DuckDB handles it, not the LLM context)
- Every result is reproducible (you have the SQL)
- Every result is verifiable (the user can read the SQL)

This is "show your work" from the correctness slide, implemented as architecture.
:::

## Works everywhere {.center}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Shiny ✨
Best experience — native reactivity.
`qc.df()` drives the whole dashboard.
:::
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Streamlit
```{.python}
from querychat.streamlit \
    import QueryChat
```
:::
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Dash
```{.python}
from querychat.dash \
    import QueryChat
```
:::
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Gradio
```{.python}
from querychat.gradio \
    import QueryChat
```
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
Same `QueryChat` API — swap the import, keep your code.
:::

::: notes
Brief slide. querychat supports four frameworks with the same core API. Shiny
gives the best experience because `qc.df()` is a true reactive value — in
Streamlit/Dash/Gradio, the integration is more manual. But the same QueryChat
constructor and tools work everywhere.

Don't dwell — 30 seconds max. The point is: you're not locked in.
:::

## Data sources {.center}

querychat works with data you already have:

```{.python}
import pandas as pd
from sqlalchemy import create_engine

# In-memory DataFrames (pandas, polars, pyarrow)
qc = QueryChat(pd.read_csv("sales.csv"), "sales")

# Databases (via SQLAlchemy)
engine = create_engine("duckdb:///warehouse.duckdb")
qc = QueryChat(engine, "transactions")

# Pins boards (shared data)
qc = QueryChat(board, "monthly_metrics")
```

::: notes
Show the breadth of data sources. For the tutorial exercises, they'll use
in-memory DataFrames (simplest). But in production, the database path is
powerful — you point querychat at your data warehouse and the LLM writes SQL
against real tables.

Under the hood, DataFrame inputs are loaded into an in-memory DuckDB for SQL
execution. This means even pandas DataFrames get DuckDB's query performance.
:::

## 🛠️ Hands-on: build your own analytics app {.center}

**~15 minutes** — work through as many as you can:

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}

### 1️⃣ Your own data

Swap the Titanic dataset for **your own CSV** (or one we provide).

Add a `data_description` to help the LLM.

:::
::: {.column width="33%"}

### 2️⃣ Add web search

Register `tool_web_search()` so the assistant can answer questions
**beyond** the dataset.

:::
::: {.column width="34%"}

### 3️⃣ Add a custom tool

Write a tool that does something the built-in tools can't — e.g., export
to CSV, send a summary email, etc.

:::
:::

Exercise file: **`exercises/03-querychat-starter.py`**

::: notes
The exercise file starts with a working querychat app on the Titanic dataset.
Task 1 is a data swap — if they don't have their own CSV, have a few
interesting ones ready (e.g., seaborn tips, Palmer penguins, a Kaggle dataset).

Task 2 adds web search — this extends the assistant beyond the dataset. The
pattern is: get `qc.client()` and call `register_tool(tool_web_search())`.

Task 3 is open-ended. Ideas: a tool that saves the current filter as a
bookmark, a tool that generates a matplotlib chart, a tool that exports the
filtered data to CSV.

Float around and help. Common stumbles:
- table_name must be alphanumeric (no hyphens or spaces)
- visualize tool requires `pip install "querychat[viz]"`
- Adding tools to a Shiny querychat app requires getting the client via
  qc.client() before the app starts
:::
